Artificial Intelligence

UX

UX Design

UX Research

Design

9 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Astronaut Richard H. Truly, STS-2 pilot, peruses some teleprinter copy
“Data driven” is a principle design has always recognized in theory, and has always struggled to apply fully in practice, because the cost of collecting, maintaining, and truly using data throughout the entire process was often too high. The first three stages of Spec Driven Design, Ingestion, Synthesis, and Specification, exist precisely to make that cost sustainable, and to do so in a way that was not available before: using artificial intelligence not as an accelerator for a single step, but as the infrastructure for the entire path, from raw data to the product’s functioning logic.
Ingestion: structuring data collection
The Ingestion stage is the moment raw material enters the process, and the first point worth clarifying concerns exactly how that material gets collected, because not all research benefits equally from the involvement of artificial intelligence, and pretending otherwise leads to collecting worse, not better.
Part of research remains, and will likely remain for a long time, purely human work. User interviews, field observations, workshops conducted side by side with the people who will actually use the product belong to this category: they require the ability to read a hesitation, to follow an unexpected thread that emerges halfway through a conversation, to build the trust needed for someone to share something they would never have written in a questionnaire. No tool, however sophisticated, replaces this kind of presence in a satisfying way, and it is not even the goal of Spec Driven Design to try. Here AI stays in a supporting role: accurate transcription, a first pass at organizing the material gathered, never conducting the interaction itself.
There is another part of research, though, that benefits enormously from having AI lead the way, not just support it. Desk research, competitive analysis, mapping market trends, reading industry reports belong to this second category: these are tasks that require moving through large volumes of published material, spotting recurring patterns across different sources, and synthesizing information quickly at a scale no single researcher could read in full within the time a real project allows. Here AI does not support human work: it leads it, with a person steering the direction of the research and critically evaluating its results.
The central point, though, is not which of the two modes is better, because neither one is: it is that both, however different in method, need to converge on the same output. An interview conducted in person and a competitive benchmark conducted with the assistance of a language model start from radically different processes, but they need to end up recorded in the same format, otherwise the result is two archives speaking different languages: one made of discursive transcripts and scattered notes, the other made of automatically generated summaries, both hard to relate to each other further down the line.
That common format is markdown. It is not an arbitrary choice: markdown is plain text, readable by anyone without dedicated software, but it is also, by construction, the kind of text language models are trained on in enormous quantity, and therefore understand and produce natively. A markdown file can open with an explicit block of metadata, such as source, date, collection channel, confidence level, followed by the actual body of the observation, written in plain prose but organized into distinct units, each with its own heading. Whether the observation comes from an interview transcribed by hand by a researcher or from a summary produced by a language model during a desk research session, what ends up archived has the same structure, and this is what makes it possible to treat, uniformly in the next stage, data that was born through completely different processes.
From collection to a knowledge base
Recording data in a shared format is the necessary condition, but it is not yet sufficient: for that material to become genuinely useful, it needs to flow into a knowledge base that grows over time, instead of remaining a collection of isolated files nobody rereads once they are archived.
This is the role of RAG, retrieval augmented generation, applied here to one’s own base of markdown notes. A RAG system breaks down the collected documents into fragments and makes them retrievable in response to a specific query, and the quality of that retrieval depends directly on the work done during collection: a corpus of markdown notes already organized into distinct units, with consistent metadata, lends itself to precise retrieval, because every fragment returned is already a complete unit of meaning, not an arbitrary cut mid sentence. Thanks to this connection, every new note collected, whether it comes from an interview or a desk research session, gets automatically related to everything gathered previously, and contradictions between old and new data surface at the moment of retrieval, instead of staying hidden until someone happens to notice them.

Example of a Structured LLM Wiki
On top of this is built what can be called an LLM wiki: a structured, cross referenced set of the notes collected, meant to be queried by a language model on a single precise point, without having to traverse the entire corpus every time a detail is needed, and updated incrementally as new research arrives, whether gathered through traditional methods or with AI assistance. It differs from a traditional archive built for a person’s occasional consultation: here every note is a node connected to other nodes, every update enriches the whole network instead of staying isolated in a new file, and the entire base becomes, over time, something to address with precise questions, rather than a place to search hoping to find something.
On top of all this still sits a choice that needs to be made carefully: which tool to use for which type of collection. A tool built for exploratory search, with web access and the ability to cite up to date sources, is the natural choice for desk research and competitive analysis. A tool built for deep reasoning over long documents, capable of maintaining coherence across large amounts of text, is the natural choice when it comes to analyzing in depth the transcripts of ten interviews in search of recurring patterns. There is no single right tool in absolute terms: there is the right tool for the type of collection being carried out at that moment, human or AI assisted as it may be, and it still holds true that no tool produces value if its output does not eventually converge into the same shared format as everything else.
The result of an Ingestion carried out this way is not simply more data: it is a living knowledge base, fed by heterogeneous sources but recorded uniformly, already ready to be queried precisely in the next stage, instead of having to be reread and reinterpreted from scratch every time a detail is needed.
Synthesis: from raw data to operational units
If Ingestion produces abundant but still unprocessed material, Synthesis is the moment that material begins to turn into something that can actually be used for designing. This is where raw insights, still tied to the specific context in which they emerged, become a more advanced version of the data: distinct units, individually traceable, and already oriented toward design.
Three categories of output characterize this stage, and each has a precise role.
Touchpoints identify the points of contact between the user and the product: every moment in which a person interacts, directly or indirectly, with what is being built. A touchpoint is not yet a function, and it is not yet an interface: it is the recognition that, at a certain moment in the user’s journey, there is an occasion for interaction the product needs to handle. Correctly identifying touchpoints, starting from the raw data gathered in Ingestion, means precisely mapping where the product needs to be present, before even deciding what it needs to do there.
Features grow out of this mapping: they are the concrete capabilities the product must offer to respond to what happens at each touchpoint. Unlike how features are often defined in traditional design, that is by intuition or by comparison with competitors, here every feature has a traceable origin: one can always trace back which touchpoint requires it, and which piece of research data made that touchpoint evident. This does not remove the designer’s judgment about which features to build and which not to, but it anchors that judgment to something verifiable, instead of leaving it to float on personal sensibility alone.
User stories complete the picture by translating features into a format that explicitly describes the user’s need and the value they expect to get, not just the technical function to be built. A well written user story at this stage does not simply state what the system must do: it states who has that need, in what circumstance, and why that capability matters to them. This is the format that makes it possible, further down the line, to check whether a design choice actually addresses the original need or has drifted away from it along the way.
The work of Synthesis is therefore a work of progressive translation: from raw, situated data, to the touchpoints that derive from it, to the features that respond to those touchpoints, to the user stories that articulate the need behind each feature. Every step stays connected to the one before it, and this is what distinguishes this synthesis from the traditional one, where the researcher’s interpretation often became an isolated artifact, hard to trace precisely back to the data that had generated it.
Specification: the bridge toward design
With Specification comes the step that closes this first part of the process and opens directly onto actual design work. This is where touchpoints, features, and user stories get transformed into prompts that describe the product’s functioning logic, and the word logic here should be understood in the most literal sense possible: what the product does, in what sequence, in response to which conditions, without yet addressing how it will be technically built.
This is a point worth being precise about, because it is easy to confuse a specification of this kind with a technical document. It is not one. A prompt written at this stage does not mention which programming language will be used, does not describe the backend architecture, does not specify which database will hold the data. It describes only the logic: when a user performs a certain action, what happens; which states a given interface element can take on; which conditions determine which behavior. It is a purely functional description, deliberately stripped of implementation details, because its purpose is not to say how to build the product, but what the product needs to do.
This choice is not incidental, and it is here that it becomes clear why this stage acts as a direct bridge toward design rather than toward development. In traditional design, the wireframe served exactly this function: communicating a product’s structure and behavior without yet committing to visual style or technical detail. A wireframe would say “here is a button that leads to this screen,” without saying how that button would be drawn or which technology would make it work. The specification produced at this stage serves the same conceptual function, but with a level of detail a traditional wireframe could never reach.
A classic wireframe, however accurate, remains a static artifact: it shows screens and arrows, but it cannot precisely describe every logical condition, every edge case, every variation in behavior depending on system state. A carefully written prompt at this stage c
